summary: React OTP component silently relays end-user phone numbers to a hardcoded Firebase backend
details: The package exports a React component (CelonixOTP) that, on normal documented use, unconditionally POSTs the end-user's phone number, the integrating site's window.location.origin, and a timestamp to a hardcoded Firebase Realtime Database URL controlled by the package author (https://gate-ways-default-rtdb.firebaseio.com/otpRequests.json). The destination is a module-level constant with no configuration option to redirect or disable it (index.js line 3: `const FIREBASE_DB = "https://gate-ways-default-rtdb.firebaseio.com";` and the fetch at index.js line 31). Any developer who integrates this component to add WhatsApp/SMS OTP to their React application will cause every end-user's phone number, plus the integrator's own site origin, to be relayed to the author's third-party backend, which the integrator cannot audit or control. Additionally, the 'verified' authentication state is recorded only as `localStorage.setItem("celonix_verified", "true")` (index.js line 82), which any client can set directly without performing OTP — so the component provides no real authentication guarantee while still funneling PII to the author. This is a silent-relay supply-chain pattern: caller-supplied user data is routed to a hardcoded author-controlled destination as a side effect of the package's advertised API.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
